slides.content: What is Information Retrieval?

slides.title: What is Information Retrieval?
slides.content:
Definition

Information Retrieval (IR) is finding material (usually documents) of an unstructured nature (usually text) that satisfies an information need from within large collections (usually stored on computers).

The Essence of IR: Given an Information Need

  • Find relevant results that satisfy the information need
  • By searching over a large collection of items (billions of documents)
  • In reasonable time:
  • Web search: $< 1$ second
  • AI/conversational systems: allow more time for generation
• Present results in a manner that is easily consumable by humans

slides.title: IR vs. Database Systems
slides.content:
AspectDatabase SystemsIR Systems
DataStructured (tables, schemas)Unstructured (text, documents)
QueriesPrecise (SQL)Vague (natural language)
MatchingExactApproximate/Ranked
ResultAll matching recordsTop-k relevant documents
FailureNo results or all resultsRanked list (best effort)
ExampleSELECT * WHERE age>30``climate change effects''

Key Difference

Databases: ``Give me all records matching X'' IR: ``Give me the most relevant documents about X''

slides.title: IR vs. Natural Language Processing (NLP)
slides.left_content:
NLP (Language Centric)
  • Focus on language understanding, representation, and generation.
  • Deep analysis of syntax, semantics, and pragmatics.
  • Models like BERT, Llama, GPT.
slides.right_content:
IR (Scale & Need Centric)
  • Focus on information seeking at a massive scale.
  • Uses NLP tech as a toolkit to model relevance.
  • Priority: Speed, ranking, and satisfying user needs.

slides.title: Examples of Search Systems
slides.content:
Conversational AI Search[0.5em] ChatGPT - AI-powered conversational search E-Commerce Search[0.5em] Amazon - Product search and recommendations

Common Thread

All search systems share the same core challenge: finding relevant items from massive collections in real-time.

slides.title: Examples of Search Systems (Figure)
Real-World IR Applications
slides.left_content:
Web Search
  • Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo
  • Billions of web pages
  • Sub-second response time
Enterprise Search
  • Search company documents
  • Email search
  • Knowledge management
slides.right_content:
E-commerce
  • Amazon product search
  • eBay, Etsy
  • Recommendation systems
Specialized Domains
  • Legal document search (LexisNexis)
  • Academic papers (Google Scholar)
  • Medical records (PubMed)
  • News archives

slides.content: Challenges in IR

slides.title: The Retrieval Challenge: Understanding Intent
slides.content:
The Semantic Gap

  • Syntactic match: Docs containing ``mouse'', ``trap'', ``poison''.
  • User intent: Docs describing humane traps or mechanical traps.
  • Danger: Returning docs about ``how poison traps work'' (contains keywords!).

slides.title: The Retrieval Challenge: Understanding Intent (Figure)
The Scale of Modern IR
slides.content:
Web Search Statistics (2024)

  • Google: Processes 8.5+ billion searches per day
  • Index size: 100+ trillion web pages indexed
  • Freshness: New pages indexed within hours
  • Speed: Results in $< 0.5$ seconds on average

Challenges at Scale

  • Storage: How to store 100 trillion documents?
  • Speed: How to search in milliseconds?
  • Relevance: How to rank billions of results?
  • Freshness: How to keep index up-to-date?
  • Quality: How to fight spam and misinformation?

slides.title: The Information Retrieval Problem
slides.content:
Core Questions:
  1. How to represent documents and queries?
  2. How to match queries to documents efficiently?
  3. How to rank documents by relevance?

slides.title: The Information Retrieval Problem (Figure)
The Information Retrieval Process
slides.content:
Forward Reference: Lecture 2

The fundamental IR process, including pipeline architectures and preprocessing, is now covered in detail in Lecture 2: Indexing.

Why the change?

  • Better logical flow: Preprocessing is tightly coupled with building indexes.
  • Focus for today: Understanding the models (Boolean and Vector Space).
Proceeding to Section 3: Boolean Retrieval...
Boolean Retrieval

slides.title: Boolean Retrieval Model
slides.content:
Definition

Documents are retrieved if they satisfy a Boolean expression of query terms
Boolean Operators:
  • AND: Both terms must appear (intersection)
  • OR: Either term must appear (union)
  • NOT: Term must not appear (negation)

Example Queries

  • climate AND change → docs containing both ``climate'' and ``change''
  • cat OR dog → docs containing ``cat'' or ``dog'' or both
  • java NOT coffee → docs with ``java'' but not ``coffee''
  • (climate AND change) OR (global AND warming)

slides.title: Boolean Retrieval with Sets
slides.left_content:
Document Collection:
  • D1: \{cat, sat, mat\}
  • D2: \{dog, sat, log\}
  • D3: \{cat, dog, run\}
  • D4: \{bird, fly, sky\}

slides.title: Boolean Retrieval Example
slides.content:
Query: (cat AND mat) OR dog Execution:
  1. cat: \{D1, D3\}
  2. mat: \{D1\}
  3. cat AND mat: \{D1, D3\} $\cap$ \{D1\} = \{D1\}
  4. dog: \{D2, D3\}
  5. (cat AND mat) OR dog: \{D1\} $\cup$ \{D2, D3\} = \{D1, D2, D3\}

slides.title: Boolean Retrieval Example (Figure)
Advantages of Boolean Retrieval
slides.content:
✓ Advantages

    [✓] Precise control: Users specify exactly what they want [✓] Transparent: Clear why documents matched [✓] Efficient: Fast set operations on posting lists [✓] Predictable: Same query always returns same results [✓] Expressive: Can construct complex queries

Use Cases

  • Legal document search (very precise requirements)
  • Patent search (need exact specifications)
  • Database-like queries on text
  • Expert users who know what they want

slides.title: Limitations of Boolean Retrieval
slides.content:
{55

Critical Limitations}
    [] No ranking
    • All matching documents equally relevant
    • Can't distinguish ``very relevant'' from ``barely relevant''
    [] All or nothing
    • climate AND change AND effects: No results if one term missing
    • Too restrictive or too permissive
    [] Hard to formulate queries
    • Most users don't know Boolean operators
    • Hard to express fuzzy information needs
    [] Result set size unpredictable
    • Could return 0 results or 10 million
    • Users want ``top 10'', not ``all matching''

slides.title: Boolean Retrieval: The Feast or Famine Problem
slides.left_content:
Too Restrictive (Famine)
  • Query: machine AND learning AND neural AND networks
  • Result: 0 documents
  • Problem: One term missing kills entire result
Solution?
  • User tries: machine AND learning
slides.right_content:
Too Permissive (Feast)
  • Query: machine AND learning
  • Result: 1,000,000 documents
  • Problem: Which 10 should I read?
Need:
  • Ranking by relevance
  • Graceful degradation
  • Better matching

slides.content: The Vector Space Model

slides.title: From Boolean to Vector Space
slides.left_content:
Boolean Model:
  • D1: \{cat, dog\}
  • D2: \{cat, mat\}
  • Binary: term present or not
Query: cat AND dog
  • Result: \{D1\}
  • D2 excluded (no ``dog'')
slides.right_content:
Vector Space Model:
  • D1: [cat: 0.5, dog: 0.5, mat: 0]
  • D2: [cat: 0.7, dog: 0, mat: 0.3]
  • Weighted: importance/frequency
Query: [cat: 0.5, dog: 0.5]
  • Similarity(D1, Q) = 0.5
  • Similarity(D2, Q) = 0.35
  • Ranked: D1, then D2

slides.title: Vector Space Model: Geometric View
slides.content: Documents and queries as vectors in term space Similarity = Angle between vectors (Smaller angle = more similar)

slides.title: Vector Space Model: Geometric View (Figure)
Term Weighting in Vector Space Model
slides.content:
Question: How to assign weights to terms?

Option 1: Binary Weights

$$w_{t,d} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if term } t \text{ appears in doc } d \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
  • Simple but ignores frequency

Option 2: Term Frequency (TF)

$$w_{t,d} = \text{count}(t, d)$$
  • Better: reflects importance
  • Problem: Common words get high weight

Problem with Raw TF

Document mentioning ``the'' 100 times doesn't make it about ``the''

slides.title: TF-IDF Weighting
slides.content:
Idea: Balance term frequency with term rarity

TF-IDF Formula

$$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \times \text{idf}(t)$$ where:
  • $\text{tf}(t,d) = \text{count}(t,d)$ (term frequency in document)
  • $\text{idf}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ (inverse document frequency)
  • $N$ = total number of documents
  • $\text{df}(t)$ = number of documents containing term $t$
Intuition:
  • High TF: Term appears often in this document → important for this doc
  • High IDF: Term is rare across collection → discriminative
  • High TF-IDF: Important AND discriminative

slides.title: TF-IDF Example
slides.content:
Collection: 10,000 documents
Termtf(t,d)df(t)idf(t)tf-idf
neural5100$\log(10000/100) = 2.0$10.0
network3500$\log(10000/500) = 1.3$3.9
the509000$\log(10000/9000) = 0.05$2.5
Observations:
  • ``neural'' appears less than ``the'' (5 vs. 50) but gets higher weight (10.0 vs. 2.5)
  • Rare, discriminative terms weighted higher
  • Common words automatically downweighted

Note

This is a simplified version; we'll cover refined TF-IDF and BM25 in Lecture 3

slides.title: Document Vectors with TF-IDF
slides.content:
Example Collection:
  • D1: ``cat cat dog''
  • D2: ``cat mat''
  • D3: ``dog bird bird''
Vocabulary: \{cat, dog, mat, bird\}
Doccatdogmatbird
D12 × log(3/2) = 0.351 × log(3/2) = 0.1800
D21 × log(3/2) = 0.1801 × log(3/1) = 0.480
D301 × log(3/2) = 0.1802 × log(3/1) = 0.95
Vector Representation:
  • D1: [0.35, 0.18, 0, 0]
  • D2: [0.18, 0, 0.48, 0]
  • D3: [0, 0.18, 0, 0.95]

slides.title: Cosine Similarity
slides.content:
How to measure similarity between vectors?

Cosine Similarity Formula

$$\text{sim}(\vec{q}, \vec{d}) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{|\vec{q}| \times |\vec{d}|} = \frac{\sum_{t} q_t \times d_t}{\sqrt{\sum_{t} q_t^2} \times \sqrt{\sum_{t} d_t^2}}$$
Properties:
  • Range: [-1, 1] (or [0, 1] for positive weights)
  • 1 = identical vectors (same direction)
  • 0 = orthogonal (no common terms)
  • -1 = opposite vectors (not relevant for IR)
Why cosine instead of dot product?
  • Cosine normalizes for document length
  • Long documents don't automatically score higher

slides.title: VSM vs. Boolean Retrieval
slides.content:
AspectBooleanVector Space
MatchingExact (in/out)Partial (weighted)
RankingNoYes (by similarity)
Result sizeUnpredictableTop-$k$
Term weightingBinaryTF-IDF
Query expressionBoolean operatorsTerm list
RobustnessBrittleGraceful degradation
User friendlinessExpert usersGeneral users
RelevanceAll equalRanked by relevance

Modern Usage

  • Boolean: Still used in legal, patent search (precision critical)
  • Vector Space: Foundation for web search, most IR systems
  • Hybrid: Many systems combine both approaches

slides.title: Advantages of Vector Space Model
slides.content:
✓ Key Advantages

  1. Ranking by relevance
    • Documents ordered by similarity to query
    • Users get best results first
  2. Partial matching
    • Documents matching some query terms still retrieved
    • Graceful degradation (no feast/famine)
  3. Term weighting
    • Important terms weighted higher (TF-IDF)
    • Automatic handling of common words
  4. Simple queries
    • Users just type terms (no Boolean operators)
    • Natural for web search
  5. Tunable
    • Can adjust weighting schemes
    • Foundation for more advanced models (BM25, Lecture 3)

slides.title: Limitations of Vector Space Model
slides.content:
{55

Limitations}
  1. Bag of words assumption
    • Word order ignored: ``dog bites man'' = ``man bites dog''
    • No phrase matching
  2. Term independence
    • Assumes terms are independent
    • ``New York'' treated as ``New'' and ``York''
  3. Synonymy
    • ``car'' and ``automobile'' treated as different
    • Misses relevant documents with synonyms
  4. Polysemy
    • ``bank'' (financial) vs. ``bank'' (river) undistinguished
    • Can retrieve irrelevant documents
Looking Ahead
